items.q: Must a model be a full-size working version of the product?
items.correct: No - models can be sketches, virtual CAD files or simple scaled forms; each type only needs enough detail to answer the design question being asked.
items.misconception: Yes - a model only counts if it is full-size and fully working.
items.random_wrong: Yes - models must always be made of the final material.
items.why: Students conflate models with prototypes. Cheap low-fidelity models earn their keep early, exploring ideas; working prototypes come later when function must be proven.

items.q: Has CAD made physical modelling unnecessary?
items.correct: No - CAD is powerful for accuracy and virtual testing, but physical models are still needed to judge ergonomics, feel, weight and real user interaction.
items.misconception: Yes - everything a physical model shows can be seen on screen.
items.random_wrong: Yes - physical models are now illegal in commercial design.
items.why: Screens cannot convey grip, balance or comfort. Professional studios combine virtual and physical modelling because each reveals problems the other misses.

items.q: In a 1:5 scale model, is the model five times bigger than the product?
items.correct: No - 1:5 means one unit on the model represents five on the product, so the model is one fifth of the real size.
items.misconception: Yes - the second number says how many times bigger the model is.
items.random_wrong: 1:5 means the model took five hours to build.
items.why: Ratio order trips students up. The first number is the model, the second the reality; 5:1 would be an enlarged model, used for small products like earbuds.

items.q: The main purpose of modelling in design is to:
items.correct: Test and communicate design ideas cheaply before committing to production
items.misconception: Replace the need for any final product
items.why: It's Test and communicate design ideas cheaply before committing to production. “Replace the need for any final product” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A key advantage of CAD modelling over physical modelling is:
items.correct: Designs can be modified and virtually tested without consuming physical material
items.why: Correct answer: Designs can be modified and virtually tested without consuming physical material. “It never contains any errors” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Rapid prototyping is best described as:
items.correct: Producing physical parts directly from CAD data, for example by 3D printing
items.misconception: Sketching very quickly with a pen
items.random_wrong: Building production tooling for mass manufacture
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Producing physical parts directly from CAD data, for example by 3D printing. “Sketching very quickly with a pen” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the difference between an aesthetic model and a functional prototype?
items.correct: An aesthetic model shows look and form; a functional prototype tests how the design works
items.misconception: There is no difference between them
items.random_wrong: An aesthetic model tests how it works; a functional prototype tests how it looks
items.why: The answer examiners want is: An aesthetic model shows look and form; a functional prototype tests how the design works. “There is no difference between them” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A scale model is built at 1:5. The real product is 1.2 m tall. How tall is the model in centimetres?
items.why: Exact answer: 24 cm. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
